rows.answer: No. A harmonic (1,1) form represents a real (1,1) cohomology class, but "rational Hodge class" implies the class lies in \(H^{1,1}(X,\mathbb{Q}) \cap H^2(X,\mathbb{Q})\). Without knowing the class is rational, the caption overclaims.

The Hodge conjecture asserts that every rational (p,p)-class is represented by an algebraic cycle, but this is not proven in general. The given class α lies in the intersection of rational cohomology and Hodge theory, but the existence of an algebraic cycle representative is not guaranteed without further assumptions (e.g., the Hodge conjecture for fourfolds). Thus, the "register ladder" (Hodge conjecture) does not *a priori* supply an algebraic cycle body for α.

In \(\mathbb{P}^n\), the **body** is the codimension-\(k\) linear subspace \(\mathbb{P}^{n-k}\) (an algebraic cycle of dimension \(n-k\)), the **shadow** is its cohomology class \([\mathbb{P}^{n-k}] \in H^{2k}(\mathbb{P}^n, \mathbb{Q})\) (a rational \((k,k)\)-class), and the **theorem-defined direction** is the correspondence between the algebraic cycle and its cohomology class (via the cycle class map). The harmonic representative is the Poincaré dual of the fundamental class of \(\mathbb{P}^{n-k}\).

Yes, for a smooth projective curve \( X \), the Hodge structure on \( H^1(X, \mathbb{Z}) \) is a natural starting point for testing algebraicity. The Hodge conjecture predicts that the rational \((1,1)\)-classes in \( H^2(X, \mathbb{Q}) \) correspond to algebraic cycles, but for \( H^1 \), the Hodge decomposition \( H^1(X, \mathbb{C}) = H^{1,0} \oplus H^{0,1} \) is purely transcendental—no nontrivial rational \((1,0)\)- or \((0,1)\)-classes are algebraic. Thus, \( H^1 \) serves as a "shadow" whose algebraic body (e.g., via Abel-Jacobi or cycle class maps) must be carefully analyzed.

rows.target_register: R3->R4 in the known p=1 case
rows.answer: The answer key can name the **Néron–Severi group** \( \mathrm{NS}(X) \), which consists of rational (1,1) classes modulo numerical equivalence. It must not generalize to arbitrary higher-dimensional varieties without assuming the Lefschetz standard conjecture or Hodge conjecture, as the correspondence between rational (p,p) classes and algebraic cycles is not known in general.

No. On a smooth projective surface, every rational (1,1) class is represented by an algebraic 1-cycle (by the Lefschetz (1,1) theorem), and such a class is the cohomology class of a divisor if and only if the 1-cycle is effective. Thus, the distinction between curve classes and divisor classes is classical and not a new Hodge phenomenon.

No, a rational (2,2) class represented by a curve class does not necessarily upgrade to an integral statement. The integral class would require the curve class to be integral, which is not guaranteed by rationality alone. The distinction between rational and integral classes is non-trivial in general.

No. The Hodge conjecture asserts that every rational (p,p)-class is represented by an algebraic cycle, but this is not guaranteed. The harmonic representative of a rational (p,p)-class need not be algebraic, so the generator cannot reuse the Hodge card without verifying algebraicity.

The algebraicity of the locus where a fixed class stays of type (p,p) does not, in general, directly construct the algebraic cycle itself. While the locus is algebraic by the Hodge conjecture for families (or more precisely, by the theorem of Cattani, Deligne, and Kaplan), this only ensures that the set of parameters where the class remains (p,p) is an algebraic subset. Constructing the actual algebraic cycle requires additional geometric or cohomological arguments, such as those provided by the Lefschetz theorem on (1,1)-classes or the Hodge conjecture in specific cases. The algebraicity of t

The repair should be to replace "the cycle" with "a representative of the (1,1) class." A cycle is a formal sum of subvarieties, not a smooth differential form. The highlighted region is a smooth representative of the cohomology class, not the class itself.
